ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for lassi; A0FKP (fermented milk drink) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for lassi as a specific food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of lassi is approximately 250 g (one glass), as commonly served in Indian restaurants and food stalls in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Lassi (Dahi Sharbat) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods reports values for plain lassi (dahi-based drink). Values represent a standard sweet lassi prepared from whole-milk yogurt diluted with water and sugar. Per-100g values are derived from the ICMR-NIN table entry for lassi/dahi sharbat. Where ICMR-NIN does not report a micronutrient, USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Yogurt, plain, whole milk' (FDC ID 171284) was used as a secondary reference for micronutrient estimation, and those fields are marked -1 where not reported.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for lassi exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 36 is estimated from the closest analogous food: plain whole-milk yogurt (GI ~36, low category), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Lassi is a diluted, blended yogurt drink; its GI is expected to be similar to or slightly lower than plain yogurt due to dilution with water. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (36 × 21.25) / 100 ≈ 7.7, based on 250 g serving with 8.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~40 mg per 100 g); potassium and calcium present
hypertension.guidance: Lassi has a low sodium content of approximately 40 mg per 100 g and provides potassium (~140 mg) and calcium (~110 mg), which are beneficial for blood pressure regulation; individuals with hypertension may consume plain or lightly salted lassi in moderate portions, but should avoid high-salt variants.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar in sweet lassi raises glycemic load; low GI base from yogurt
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain or lightly sweetened lassi has a low glycemic index (~36) due to its yogurt base, but sweet lassi with added sugar can contribute significant carbohydrate load (~8.5 g per 100 g, GL ~7.7 per 250 g serving); individuals with type 2 diabetes should prefer unsweetened or minimally sweetened versions and monitor portion size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from whole-milk yogurt; low sodium; beneficial dairy nutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lassi made from whole-milk yogurt contains approximately 1.3 g saturated fat per 100 g, which is moderate; individuals with cardiovascular disease may choose low-fat yogurt-based lassi to reduce saturated fat intake, while benefiting from the calcium, potassium, and probiotic content of the fermented dairy base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lassi is a traditional South Asian yogurt-based beverage originating from the Punjab region of the Indian subcontinent, widely consumed across India, Pakistan, and the South Asian diaspora globally, including in Singapore where it is served in Indian and Middle Eastern restaurants. It is prepared by blending or churning yogurt (dahi) with water and either sugar (sweet lassi) or salt and spices such as cumin or cardamom (salted lassi). The result is a smooth, creamy, slightly tangy drink with a refreshing quality. Mango lassi, a popular variant, incorporates fresh or canned mango pulp. Nutritionally, lassi provides moderate protein (~2.8 g per 100 g) and calcium (~110 mg per 100 g) from its yogurt base, with a low glycemic index of approximately 36, making it a relatively blood-sugar-friendly beverage compared to fruit juices or sweetened sodas. The fermented dairy base contributes probiotics that may support gut health. Sweet lassi contains added sugar and should be consumed in moderation by individuals managing type 2 diabetes or weight. Lassi is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable, and is commonly consumed as a cooling accompaniment to spicy meals or as a standalone refreshment.
common_user_queries: What is the difference between sweet lassi and salted lassi?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for lassi/dahi sharbat, which is the closest approved primary source for this Indian beverage. GI is estimated by analogy from plain whole-milk yogurt (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008) as no direct GI entry for lassi exists. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient match is adapted (not an exact entry for the specific restaurant-served product) and the GI is an analogous estimate.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on ICMR-NIN data for plain sweet lassi (dahi sharbat) and supplemented with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 171284, plain whole-milk yogurt) for micronutrients not reported by ICMR-NIN. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for lassi; USDA data for plain yogurt reports a negligible amount not reliably transferable to this adapted entry. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for lassi. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for lassi. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero or trace per USDA FoodData Central for plain whole-milk yogurt; naturally zero in unprocessed dairy-based beverages. 6. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as lassi is a liquid dairy beverage with no plant fibre components in the plain variant. 7. GI of 36 is an analogous estimate from plain whole-milk yogurt (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI entry for lassi exists in the approved GI database. GL per 250 g serving = (36 × 8.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 7.65, rounded to 7.7. 8. Input cuisine tag was 'Middle Eastern' (from field survey restaurant context); canonical cuisine corrected to 'Indian' as lassi is an Indian subcontinent beverage; the serving restaurants include both Middle Eastern and Indian establishments. 9. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this food is served in Singapore. 10. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311434 is used for lassi as a fermented dairy beverage; this should be verified against the live FoodOn ontology. 11. FoodEx2 code A0FKP (fermented milk drink) is used as the closest defensible category anchor; no dedicated lassi code exists in FoodEx2.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full record. Identified that cuisine input tag 'Middle Eastern' needed correction to canonical 'Indian'. Applied Singaporean multilingual naming rules (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Populated all nutrient fields using ICMR-NIN as primary source. Set GI by analogy from plain yogurt with full documentation. Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list. Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes. Confirmed dietary_fibre_g=0.0 and trans_fat_g=0.0 with justification phrases in notes.

Iteration 2: Validator feedback indicated REJECT due to g5_ontology_honesty: ontology.foodex2_match_type must be one of ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']. The value 'analogous_estimate' was incorrect. Corrected foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent' as A0FKP (fermented milk drink) is the closest parent category for lassi.

Iteration 3: Verified all hard gates and Q1-Q8 criteria after correcting foodex2_match_type. All hard gates now pass, and score remains 8/8. Confirmed aliases consistency, zero-macro justification, and sentinel value documentation. Decision: PASS.
